title: Listening - Part 3: Helping at an Event
description: You are going to hear five extracts in which people talk about their experience of helping at an event. For questions 19–23, choose from the list A–H what each speaker found most rewarding about helping. Use the letters once only. There are three letters that you do not need to use.
instructions: You are going to hear five extracts in which people talk about their experience of helping at an event. For questions 19–23, choose from the list A–H what each speaker found most rewarding about helping. Use the letters once only. There are three letters that you do not need to use.

extracts.context: Five people talk about helping to run an event.
extracts.audio_script: Speaker 1 Woman: I helped at our town's children's book festival, setting out chairs and guiding families to different rooms. The work itself was fairly ordinary, and I didn't meet any famous authors. [19]The best part was watching children arrive looking uncertain and then leave excitedly holding books, telling their parents about the stories and characters they had discovered.[/19] I realised that an event can make reading seem like an adventure rather than homework. I have already volunteered for next year's festival.||Speaker 2 Man: During a charity concert at college, I was selling tickets at the entrance. About twenty minutes before the show, heavy rain started and water began coming into the outdoor waiting area. [20]I was proud that our small volunteer team quickly found an indoor queueing space, moved signs and got everyone inside safely without delaying the performance.[/20] I'd worried I would be useless if anything went wrong, so successfully dealing with the situation gave me far more satisfaction than simply completing my original task.||Speaker 3 Woman: I moved to the area shortly before the summer food festival and didn't know many people. I agreed to help at an information stall partly because I had nothing else planned. [21]During the weekend I worked alongside volunteers of different ages, chatted to stallholders and was invited to join a local walking group by people I met there.[/21] The festival was enjoyable, but the important result for me was that I stopped feeling like a stranger in the town and began to develop a social life.||Speaker 4 Man: Our school held a concert, and I volunteered to help with the sound equipment even though I'd never touched a mixing desk. A technician explained what each control did during rehearsals. [22]By the end of the evening, I could set up microphones and adjust sound levels myself, which felt like gaining a practical ability I could use for future school performances.[/22] The concert went well, which was a relief, but I most enjoyed discovering that technical work behind the stage was something I might be good at.||Speaker 5 Woman: At the sports day for younger pupils, I expected to carry equipment or hand out water. Instead, a teacher asked me to manage the starting area for several races, checking names and making sure groups were ready on time. [23]I liked the fact that the staff relied on me to organise something important without constantly supervising me, and that I managed it correctly.[/23] The children were energetic and sometimes difficult to control, but being given real responsibility made the day much more meaningful than I'd expected.

dialogue.speaker: speaker_1
dialogue.text: I helped at our town's children's book festival, setting out chairs and guiding families to different rooms. The work itself was fairly ordinary, and I didn't meet any famous authors. The best part was watching children arrive looking uncertain and then leave excitedly holding books, telling their parents about the stories and characters they had discovered. I realised that an event can make reading seem like an adventure rather than homework. I have already volunteered for next year's festival.

dialogue.speaker: speaker_2
dialogue.text: During a charity concert at college, I was selling tickets at the entrance. About twenty minutes before the show, heavy rain started and water began coming into the outdoor waiting area. I was proud that our small volunteer team quickly found an indoor queueing space, moved signs and got everyone inside safely without delaying the performance. I'd worried I would be useless if anything went wrong, so successfully dealing with the situation gave me far more satisfaction than simply completing my original task.

dialogue.speaker: speaker_3
dialogue.text: I moved to the area shortly before the summer food festival and didn't know many people. I agreed to help at an information stall partly because I had nothing else planned. During the weekend I worked alongside volunteers of different ages, chatted to stallholders and was invited to join a local walking group by people I met there. The festival was enjoyable, but the important result for me was that I stopped feeling like a stranger in the town and began to develop a social life.

dialogue.speaker: speaker_4
dialogue.text: Our school held a concert, and I volunteered to help with the sound equipment even though I'd never touched a mixing desk. A technician explained what each control did during rehearsals. By the end of the evening, I could set up microphones and adjust sound levels myself, which felt like gaining a practical ability I could use for future school performances. The concert went well, which was a relief, but I most enjoyed discovering that technical work behind the stage was something I might be good at.

dialogue.speaker: speaker_5
dialogue.text: At the sports day for younger pupils, I expected to carry equipment or hand out water. Instead, a teacher asked me to manage the starting area for several races, checking names and making sure groups were ready on time. I liked the fact that the staff relied on me to organise something important without constantly supervising me, and that I managed it correctly. The children were energetic and sometimes difficult to control, but being given real responsibility made the day much more meaningful than I'd expected.

options.text: raising a large amount of money

questions.speaker: Speaker 1
questions.explanation: The speaker found it rewarding to see children's enthusiasm after attending the book festival (evidence [19]).

questions.speaker: Speaker 2
questions.explanation: The speaker was pleased that the volunteer team managed an unforeseen weather-related difficulty (evidence [20]).

questions.speaker: Speaker 3
questions.explanation: Helping at the festival enabled the speaker to meet local people and begin making friends (evidence [21]).

questions.speaker: Speaker 4
questions.explanation: The speaker valued acquiring practical skills in setting up and controlling sound (evidence [22]).

questions.speaker: Speaker 5
questions.explanation: The speaker appreciated being trusted to manage an important area of the event independently (evidence [23]).
